title: DROID-SLAM: Deep Visual SLAM for Monocular, Stereo, and RGB-D Cameras [PDF]

# **DROID-SLAM: Deep Visual SLAM for Monocular, Stereo, and RGB-D Cameras**

**Zachary Teed Jia Deng** Princeton University `{zteed,jiadeng}@princeton.edu`

## **Abstract**

We introduce DROID-SLAM, a new deep learning based SLAM system. DROIDSLAM consists of recurrent iterative updates of camera pose and pixelwise depth through a Dense Bundle Adjustment layer. DROID-SLAM is accurate, achieving large improvements over prior work, and robust, suffering from substantially fewer catastrophic failures. Despite training on monocular video, it can leverage stereo or RGB-D video to achieve improved performance at test time. The URL to our open source code is `https://github.com/princeton-vl/DROID-SLAM` .

## **1 Introduction**

Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) aims to (1) build a map of the environment and (2) localize the agent within the environment. It is a special form of Structure-from-Motion (SfM) focused on accurate tracking of long-term trajectories. It is a critical capability for robotics, especially autonomous vehicles. In this work, we address _visual_ SLAM, where sensor recordings come in the form of images captured from a monocular, stereo, or RGB-D camera.

The SLAM problem has been approached from a number of different angles. Early work was built using probabilistic and filtering based approaches [12, 30], and alternating optimization of the map and camera poses [34, 16]. More recently, modern SLAM systems have leveraged least-squares optimization. A key element for accuracy has been full Bundle Adjustment (BA), which jointly optimizes the camera poses and the 3D map in a single optimization problem. One advantage of the optimization-based formulation is that a SLAM system can be easily modified to leverage different sensors. For example, ORB-SLAM3 [5] supports monocular, stereo, RGB-D, and IMU sensors, and modern systems can support a variety of camera models [5, 27, 42, 6]. Despite significant progress, current SLAM systems lack the robustness demanded for many real-world applications. Failures come in many forms, such as lost feature tracks, divergence in the optimization algorithm, and accumulation of drift.

Deep learning has been proposed as a solution to many of these failure cases. Previous work has investigated replacing hand-crafted with learned features[13, 7, 29, 26, 35], using neural 3D representations[46, 1, 9, 45, 44, 25, 22], and combining learned energy terms with classical optimization backends[58, 57]. Other work has tried to learn SLAM or VO systems end-to-end [59, 47, 53, 52, 46]. While these systems are sometimes more robust, they fall far short of the accuracy of their classical counterparts on common benchmarks.

In this work we introduce DROID-SLAM, a new SLAM system based on deep learning. It has state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing SLAM systems, classical or learning-based, on challenging benchmarks with very large margins. In particular, it has the following advantages:

- _High Accuracy_ : We achieve large improvements over prior work across multiple datasets and modalities. On the TartanAir SLAM competition [54], we reduce error by 62% over the best prior result on the monocular track and 60% on the stereo track. We rank 1st on the

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

ETH-3D RGB-D SLAM leaderboard [41], outperforming the second place by 35% under the AUC metric which considers both error and rate of catastrophic failure. On EuRoC [2], with monocular input, we reduce error by 82% among methods with zero failures, and by 43% over ORB-SLAM3 considering only the 10 out of 11 sequences it succeeds on. With stereo input, we reduce error by 71% over ORB-SLAM3. On TUM-RGBD [43], we reduce error by 83% among the methods with zero failures.

- _High Robustness_ : We have substantially fewer catastrophic failures than prior systems. On ETH-3D, we successfully track 30 of the 32 RGB-D datasets, while the next best successfully tracks only 19/32. On TartanAir, EuRoC, and TUM-RGBD, we have zero failures.

- _Strong Generalization_ : Our system, trained only with monocular input, can directly use stereo or RGB-D input to get improved accuracy without any retraining. All of our results across 4 datasets and 3 modalities are achieved by a single model, trained once with only monocular input entirely on the synthetic TartanAir dataset.

The strong performance and generalization of DROID-SLAM is made possible by its “Differentiable Recurrent Optimization-Inspired Design” (DROID), which is an end-to-end differentiable architecture that combines the strengths of both classical approaches and deep networks. Specifically, it consists of recurrent iterative updates, building upon RAFT [48] for optical flow but introducing two key innovations.

First, unlike RAFT, which iteratively updates optical flow, we iteratively update camera poses and depth. Whereas RAFT operates on two frames, our updates are applied to an arbitrary number of frames, enabling joint global refinement of all camera poses and depth maps, essential for minimizing drift for long trajectories and loop closures.

Second, each update of camera poses and depth maps in DROID-SLAM is produced by a differentiable Dense Bundle Adjustment (DBA) layer, which computes a Gauss-Newton update to camera poses and _dense per-pixel depth_ so as to maximize their compatibility with the current estimate of optical flow. This DBA layer leverages geometric constraints, improves accuracy and robustness, and enables a monocular system to handle stereo or RGB-D input without retraining.

The design of DROID-SLAM is novel. The closest prior deep architectures are DeepV2D [47] and BA-Net [46], both of which were focused on depth estimation and reported limited SLAM results. DeepV2D alternates between updating depth and updating camera poses, instead of bundle adjustment. BA-Net has a bundle adjustment layer, but their layer is substantially different: it is not “dense” in that it optimizes over a small number of coefficients used to linearly combine a depth basis (a set of pre-predicted depth maps), whereas we optimize over per-pixel depth directly, without being handicapped by a depth basis. In addition, BA-Net optimizes photometric reprojection error (in feature space), whereas we optimize geometric error, leveraging state-of-the-art flow estimation.

We perform extensive evaluation across four different datasets and three different sensor modalities, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in all cases. We also include ablation studies that shed light on important design decisions and hyperparameters.

## **2 Related Work**

Modern SLAM systems treat localization and mapping as a joint optimization problem [4].

2

**Visual SLAM** focuses on observations in the form of monocular, stereo, or RGB-D images. These approaches are commonly categorized as either being _direct_ or _indirect_ [15]. Indirect approaches [31, 32, 5, 37] first process the image into an intermediate representation by detecting points of interest and attaching feature descriptors. Features are then matched between images. Indirect approaches optimize camera pose and a 3D point cloud by minimizing reprojection error–the distance between a projected 3D point and its location in the image.

Direct approaches model the image formation process and define an objective function over photometric error [16, 15, 60]. One advantage of direct approaches is that they can model more information about the image, such as lines and intensity variations[15] which are not used by indirect approaches. However, photometric errors typically lead to more difficult optimization problems, and direct approaches are less robust to geometric distortion such as rolling shutter artifacts. This approach requires more sophisticated optimization techniques, such as coarse-to-fine image pyramids to avoid local minimum.

Our method does not clearly fit into either of the categories. Like the _direct_ approach, we do not require preprocessing steps to detect and match features between the images. We instead use the full image, allowing us to leverage a wider range of information than indirect methods with typically only use corners and edges. However, we minimize reprojection error similar to indirect methods. This is an easier optimization problem and avoids the need for more complicated representations such as image pyramids. In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.

**Deep Learning** has more recently been applied to the SLAM problem. Many works have focused on training systems for particular subproblems, such as feature detection [13, 7, 29, 26, 35], feature matching and outlier rejection [38, 36], and localization [51, 39]. SuperGlue [38] was designed to perform feature matching and verification and make 2-view pose estimate much more robust. Our network also draws inspiration from Dusmanu et al[14], which builds a neural network into the SfM pipeline to improve keypoint localization accuracy.

Other works have focused on training SLAM systems end-to-end [59, 46, 8, 50, 24, 47, 53]. These methods are not full SLAM systems, but instead focus on small scale reconstruction on the order of two [8, 50, 53] up to a dozen frames [59, 46, 47]. They lack many of the core capabilities of modern SLAM systems such as loop closure and global bundle adjustment which inhibit their ability to perform large scale reconstruction as demonstrated in our experiments. _∇_ SLAM[23] implements several existing SLAM algorithms as differentiable computation graphs, allowing for errors in the reconstruction to be backpropagated back to sensor measurements. While this approach is differentiable, it has no trainable parameters, meaning the performance of the system is limited by the accuracy of the classical algorithm they emulate.

DeepFactors[9] is the most complete deep SLAM system, building on the earlier CodeSLAM[1]. It performs joint optimization of the pose and depth variables, and is capable of short and long-range loop closure. Similar to BA-Net[46], DeepFactors optimizes the parameters of a learned depth basis during inference. In contrast, we do not rely on a learned basis, but instead optimize pixelwise depth. This allows our network to better generalize to new datasets since our depth representation is not tied to the training dataset.

We take a video as input with two objectives: estimate the trajectory of the camera and build a 3D map of the environment. We first describe the monocular setting; in Sec. 3.4 we describe how to generalize the system to stereo and RGB-D video.

## **3.1 Feature Extraction and Correlation**

Features are extracted from each new image added to they system. Key components of this stage are borrowed from RAFT[48].

**Feature Extraction** Each of the input images are processed by a feature extraction network. The network consists of 6 residual blocks and 3 downsampling layers, producing dense feature maps at 1/8 the input image resolution. Like RAFT[48], we use two separate networks: a feature network and a context network. The feature network is used to build the set of correlation volumes, while the context features are injected into the network during each application of the update operator.

**Correlation Pyramid** For each edge in the frame graph, ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ , we compute a 4D correlation volume by taking the dot product between all-pairs of feature vectors in _gθ_ ( _Ii_ ) and _gθ_ ( _Ij_ )
We then perform average pooling of the last two dimension of the correlation volume following RAFT[48] to form a 4-level correlation pyramid.

**Correlation Lookup** We define a lookup operator which indexes the correlation volume using a grid with radius _r_ , _Lr_ : R _[H][×][W][ ×][H][×][W] ×_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] _�→_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[(] _[r]_[+1)][2] .

The lookup operator takes an _H × W_ grid of coordinates as input and values are retrieved from the correlation volume using bilinear interpolation. The operator is applied to each correlation volume in the pyramid and the final feature vector is computed by concatenating the results at each level.

## **3.2 Update Operator**

The core component of our SLAM system is a learned _update operator_ show in Fig. 2. The update operator is a 3 _×_ 3 convolutional GRU with hidden state **h** . Each application of the operator updates the hidden state, and additionally produces a pose update, ∆ _**ξ**_[(] _[k]_[)] , and depth update, ∆ **d**[(] _[k]_[)] . The pose and depth updates are applied to the current depth and pose estimates through retraction on the SE3 manifold and vector addition respectively

Iterative applications of the update operator produce a sequence of poses and depths, with the expectation of converging to a fixed point _{_ **G**[(] _[k]_[)] _} →_ **G** _[∗]_ , _{_ **d**[(] _[k]_[)] _} →_ **d** _[∗]_ , reflecting the true reconstruction.

**Correspondence** At the start of each iteration we use the current estimates of poses and depths to estimate correspondence. Given a grid of pixel coordinates, **p** _i ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] in frame _i_ , we compute the dense correspondence field **p** _ij_

for each edge ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ in the frame graph. Here Π _c_ is the camera model mapping a set of 3D points onto the image and Π _[−] c_[1] is the inverse projection function mapping inverse depth map **d** and coordinate grid **p** _i_ to a 3D point cloud (we provide formulas and Jacobians in the appendix). **p** _ij_ represents the coordinates of pixels **p** _i_ mapped into frame _j_ using the estimated pose and depth.

**Inputs** We use the correspondence field to index the correlation volumes. For each edge ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ we use **p** _ij_ to perform lookup from the correlation volume **C** _ij_ to retrieve correlation features. Additionally, we use the correspondence field to derive optical flow induced by camera motion as the difference **p** _ij −_ **p** _j_ . Furthermore, the residual from the previous BA solution is concatenated with the flow field allowing the network to use feedback from the previous iteration.

The correlation features provide information about visual similarity in the neighbourhood of **p** _ij_ allowing the network to learn to align visually similar image regions. However, correspondence is sometimes ambiguous. The flow provides an complementary source of information allowing the network to exploit smoothness in the motion fields to gain robustness.

**Update** The correlation features and flow features are each mapped through two convolutional layers before being injected into the GRU. Additionally, we inject context features, as extracted by the context network, into the GRU through element-wise addition.

The ConvGRU is a local operation with a small receptive field. We extract global context by averaging the hidden state across the spatial dimensions of the image and use this feature vector as additional input to the GRU. Global context is important in SLAM because incorrect correspondences, caused by large moving objects for example, can degrade the accuracy of the system. It is important for the network to recognize and reject erroneous correspondence.

The GRU produces an updated hidden state **h**[(] _[k]_[+1)] . Instead of predicting updates to the depth or pose directly, we instead predict updates in the space of dense flow fields. We map the hidden state through two additional convoluation layers to produce two outputs: (1) a revision flow field **r** _ij ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] and (2) associated confidence map **w** _ij ∈_ R _[H]_ + _[×][W][ ×]_[2] . The revision **r** _ij_ is a correction term predicted by the network to correct errors in the dense correspondence field. We denote the corrected correspondence as **p** _[∗] ij_[=] **[ r]** _[ij]_[+] **[ p]** _[ij]_

We then pool the hidden state over all features which share the same source view _i_ and predict a pixel-wise damping factor _λ_ . We use the softplus operator to ensure that the damping term is positive. Additionally, we use the pooled features to predict a 8x8 mask which can be used to upsample the inverse depth estimate.

## **3.3 Training**

Our SLAM system is implemented in PyTorch and we use the LieTorch extension [49] to perform backprogation in the tangent space of all group elements.

5

**Removing gauge freedom** In the monocular setting, the network is only able to recover the trajectory of the camera up to a similarity transform. One solution is to define a loss which is invariant to similarity transforms. However, the gauge-freedom still exists during training which poorly impacts the conditioning of the linear system and the stability of the gradients. We solve this problem by fixing the first two poses to the ground-truth poses of each training sequence. Fixing the first pose removes the 6-dof gauge freedom. Fixing the second pose resolves the scale freedom.

**Constructing training video** Each training example consists of a 7-frame video sequence. In order to ensure stable training and good downstream performance, we want to sample videos which are not too easy nor too difficult.

The training set is composed of a collection of videos. For each video _i_ of length _Ni_ , we precompute an _Ni × Ni_ distance matrix storing the average optical flow magnitude between each pair of frames. However, not all frames are covisible; and frames pairs with less than 50% overlap are assigned a distance of infinity. During training, we dynamically generate videos by sampling paths in the distance matrix, such that the average flow between adjacent video frames is between 8px and 96px.

**Supervision** We supervise our network using a combination of _pose_ loss and _flow_ loss. The flow loss is applied to pairs of adjacent frames. We compute the optical flow induced by the predicted depth and poses and the flow induced by the ground truth depth and poses. The loss is taken to be the average l2 distance between the two flow fields.

## **3.4 SLAM System**

During inference, we compose the network into a full SLAM system. The SLAM system takes a video stream as input, and performs reconstruction and localization in real-time. Our system contains two threads which run asynchronously. The _frontend_ thread takes in new frames, extracts features, selects keyframes, and performs local bundle adjustment. The _backend_ thread simultaneously performs global bundle adjustment over the entire history of keyframes. We provide an overview of the system here, and provide more information in the appendix.

**Initialization** Initialization is simple with DROID-SLAM. We simply collect frames until we have a set of 12. As we accumulate frames, we only keep the previous frame if optical flow is greater than 16px (estimated by applying one update iteration). Once 12 frames have been accumulated, we initialize a frame graph by creating an edges between keyframes which are within 3 timesteps apart, then run 10 iterations of the update operator.

**Frontend** The frontend operates directly on the incoming video stream. It maintains a collection of keyframes and a frame graph storing edges between covisible kefyrames. Keyframe poses and depths are actively being optimized. Features are first extracted from the incoming frames. The new frame is then added to the frame graph adding edges with its 3 closest neighbors as measured by mean optical flow. The pose is initialized using a linear motion model. We then apply several iterations of the update operator to update keyframe poses and depths. We fix the first two poses to remove gauge freedom but treat all depths as free variables.

After the new frame is tracked, we select a keyframe for removal. We compute distance between pairs of frames by computing the average optical flow magnitude and remove redundant frames. If no frame is a good candidate for removal, we remove the oldest keyframe.

**Backend** The backend performs global bundle adjustment over the entire history of keyframes. During each iteration, we rebuild the frame graph using the flow between all pairs of keyframes, represented as an _N × N_ distance matrix. We first add edges between temporally adjacent keyframes. We then sample new edges from the distance matrix in order of increasing flow. With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs _||_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ ( _k, l_ ) _||∞_ = max( _|i − k|, |j − l|_ ).

We then apply the update operator to the entire frame graph, often consisting of thousands of frames and edges. Storing the full set of correlation volumes would quickly exceed video memory. Instead, we use the memory efficient implementation proposed in RAFT [48].

Figure 3: DROID-SLAM can generalize to new datasets. In order, we show results from Tanks & Temples [21], ScanNet [10], Sintel [3], and ETH-3D [41]; all using monocular video.

During training, we implement dense bundle adjustment in PyTorch to leverage the automatic differentiation engine. At inference time, we use a custom CUDA kernel which takes advantage of the block-sparse structure of the problem, then perform sparse Cholesky decomposition on the reduced camera block.

We only perform full bundle adjustment on keyframe images. In order to recover the poses of non-keyframes, we perform motion-only bundle adjustment by iteratively estimating flow between each keyframe and its neighboring non-keyframes. During testing, we evaluate on the full camera trajectory, not just keyframes.

**Stereo and RGB-D** Our system can be easily modified for stereo and RGB-D video. In the case of RGB-D, we still treat depth as a variable, since sensor depth can be noisy and have missing observations, and simply add a term to the optimization objective (Eqn. 4) which penalizes the squared distance between the measured and predicted depth. For stereo, we use the exact same system described above, with just double the frames, and fix the relative pose between the left and right frames in the DBA layer. Cross camera edges in the graph allow us to leverage stereo information.

## **4 Experiments**

We experiment on a diverse set of datasets and sensor modalities. We compare to both deep learning and established classical SLAM algorithms and put specific emphasis on cross-dataset generalization. Following prior work, we evaluate the accuracy of the camera trajectory [31, 15, 41], primarily using Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) [43]. While some datasets have ground truth point clouds [21], there is no standard protocol to compare 3D reconstructions directly given by SLAM systems because a SLAM systems can choose which 3D points to reconstruct. Evaluating dense 3D reconstruction is typically considered in the domain of Multiview Stereo [19] and outside the scope of this work.

Our network is trained entirely on monocular video from the synthetic TartanAir dataset [54]. We train our network for 250k steps with a batch size of 4, resolution 384 _×_ 512, and 7 frame clips, and unroll 15 update iterations. Training takes 1 week on 4 RTX-3090 GPUs.

Table 1: Results on the TartanAir monocular benchmark.

**TartanAir [54] (Monocular & Stereo)** The TartanAir dataset is a challenging synthetic benchmark for evaluating SLAM algorithms and was used as part of the ECCV 2020 SLAM competition. We use the official test split [54], and provide ATE across all “Hard” sequences in Tab. 1.

Tab. 1 demonstrates both the robustness of our method (no catastrophic failures) and accuracy (very low drift). We retrain DeepV2D [47] on TartanAir as a baseline. On most sequences, we outperform existing methods by an order-of-magnitude and achieve 8x lower average error than TartanVO [53] and 20x lower than DeepV2D [47]. We also use the TartanAir dataset to compare with the top submissions to the ECCV 2020 SLAM competition in Tab. 2. The top two submissions use systems

**EuRoC [2] (Monocular & Stereo)** In the remaining experiments, we are interested in the ability of our network to generalize to new cameras and environments. The EuRoC dataset consists of video captured from sensor on-board a micro aerial vehicle (MAV) and is a widely used benchmark to evaluate SLAM systems. We use the EuRoC dataset to evaluate both monocular and stereo performance and report results on Tab. 3.

In the monocular setting, we achieve an average ATE of 2.2cm, reducing error by 82% among methods with zero failures, and by 43% over ORB-SLAM3 when only comparing sequences where ORB-SLAM3 is successful.

We compare to several deep learning approaches. We compare to DeepV2D trained on the TartanAir dataset and the publicly available version trained on NYUv2 [33] and ScanNet[10]. DeepFactors [9] was trained on ScanNet. We find that recent deep learning approaches [9, 47, 53] perform poorly on the EuRoC dataset compared to classical SLAM systems. This is due to poor generalization and dataset biases which lead to large amounts of drift; our method does not suffer from these issues. D3VO [57] is able to achieve both good robustness and accuracy by combining a neural network frontend with DSO as a backend, using 6 of the 11 sequences for evaluation and performing unsupervised training on the remaining ones, which contain the same scenes used for evaluation.

**TUM-RGBD [43]** The RGBD dataset consists of indoor scenes captured with handheld camera. This is a notoriously difficult dataset for monocular methods due to rolling shutter artifacts, motion blur, and heavy rotation. We benchmark prior work on the entirety of the freiburg1 set in Tab. 4.

**ETH3D-SLAM [41] (RGB-D)** Finally, we evaluate the RGB-D performance on the ETH3D-SLAM benchmark. In this setup, the network is also provided measurements from an RGB-D camera. We take our network trained on TartanAir and add an addition term in the optimization objective penalizing the distance between the predicted inv. depth and inv. depth measured by the sensor. Without any finetuning, our method ranks 1st on both the train and test splits. Several of the datasets are "dark" meaning no image data is available; on these datasets we do not submit any predictions. On the test set, we successfully track 30/32 RGB-D, improving over the next best of 19/32.

**Timing and Memory** Our system can run in real-time with 2 3090 GPUs. Tracking and local BA is run on the first GPU, while global BA and loop closure is run on the second. On EuRoC, we average 20fps (camera hz) by downsampling to 320 _×_ 512 resolution and skipping every other frame. Results in Tab. 3 were obtained in this setting. On TUM-RGBD, we average 30fps by downsampling to 240 _×_ 320 and skipping every other frame, again the reported results where obtained in this setting. On TartanAir, due to much faster camera motion, we are unable to run in real-time, averaging 8fps. However, this is still a 16x speedup over the top 2 submissions to the TartanAir SLAM challenge, which rely on COLMAP [40].

The SLAM frontend can be run on GPUs with 8GB of memory. The backend, which requires storing feature maps from the full set of images, is more memory intensive. All results on TUM-RGBD can be produced on a single 1080Ti graphics card. Results on EuRoC, TartanAir and ETH-3D (where video can be up to 5000 frames) requires a GPU with 24GB memory. While memory and resource requirements are currently the biggest limitation of our system, we believe these can be drastically reduced by culling redundant computation and more efficient representations.

## **5 Conclusion**

We introduce DROID-SLAM, an end-to-end neural architecture for visual SLAM. DROID-SLAM is accurate, robust, and versatile and can be used on monocular, stereo, and RGB-D video. It outperforms prior work by large margins on challenging benchmarks.

While differentiability and efficient solving are plausible via sparsification (e.g., limiting co-visibility), the block diagonal assertion contradicts the stated use of global frame graphs and recurrence. This leap in reasoning is not justified, and given the centrality of DBA to the method, the explanation is too hand-wavy.

comments.title: OCR error in softplus operator description
comments.quote: We use the softplus operator to ensure that the damping term is positive.
comments.explanation: No issue with this quote. The use of softplus(·) = log(1 + exp(·)) to ensure positivity of λ is standard and correctly applied in deep networks. While the passage omits the explicit formula, this is not a flaw—softplus is widely known in ML literature. The context implies correct usage, and no ambiguity arises.

comments.title: Ambiguous resolution of 8x8 mask for depth upsampling
comments.quote: Additionally, we use the pooled features to predict a 8x8 mask which can be used to upsample the inverse depth estimate.
comments.explanation: The prediction of an ‘8x8 mask’ from pooled features is described, but its application is vague. Is this a residual update to the depth map? A learned upsampling kernel? Used in conjunction with subpixel convolutions? In RAFT, such masks are used for subpixel flow refinement via convex combinations. Here, the depth map d_t is defined at full resolution, but the update ∆d is produced at 1/8 resolution via feature maps. So the 8x8 mask might be used to upsample ∆d—yet this connection is not made. While using a small mask for upsampling is plausible (and similar to RAFT), the lack of detail—input resolution, dimensionality, application method—means a reader cannot reproduce this component. The authors correctly highlight upsampling as part of the design (important for handling full-resolution depth), but the mechanism is underspecified.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about combining direct and indirect method advantages
comments.quote: In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their method combines the best of direct and indirect approaches, which is insightful. They correctly note using full image (like direct methods) but minimizing reprojection error (like indirect). However, the sentence contains a clear typo: it says 'the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches'—but indirect methods are usually criticized for *less* modeling capacity (sparser features). The authors clearly meant 'direct'. This misstatement could mislead a reader about the method’s relationship to the taxonomy. Since this is in the Related Work section, likely due to copying or editing error, and the intended meaning is recoverable from context, it does not undermine the technical claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of Chebyshev distance suppression in backend edge sampling
comments.quote: With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs _||_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ ( _k, l_ ) _||∞_ = max( _|i − k|, |j − l|_ ).
comments.explanation: At first, I interpreted this as a standard edge pruning strategy to avoid spatial redundancy, which is common in bundle adjustment systems. The use of Chebyshev distance to define a 2D neighborhood around an edge (i,j) makes sense in principle for suppressing nearby edges in the frame graph. However, the definition of '_distance of 2_' is underspecified: does it mean a window of radius 2 (i.e., all edges (k,l) such that max(|i−k|, |j−l|) ≤ 2)? This would include edges up to 2 timesteps away in either direction, potentially over-suppressing long-range loop closures. Alternatively, is this meant to suppress only temporally adjacent edges? The context in the PAPER SUMMARY mentions keyframe selection and co-visibility but does not clarify this suppression mechanism. Given that the backend relies on long-range edges for loop closure, overly aggressive suppression could hurt performance, yet no ablation is provided. While RAFT [48] is cited for memory efficiency, that paper does not address edge suppression in this way, so this appears to be a novel heuristic without sufficient specification.

comments.title: Inconsistency in handling stereo input: 'double the frames' vs fixed inter-camera pose
comments.quote: For stereo, we use the exact same system described above, with just double the frames, and fix the relative pose between the left and right frames in the DBA layer.
comments.explanation: The statement suggests that stereo input is handled by doubling the number of frames—presumably one set for left and one for right cameras—while fixing their relative pose. However, earlier sections define the system as processing a sequential video stream with temporal indices, and the update operator and frame graph are built on temporal ordering. Introducing 'double the frames' without clarifying whether they are treated as temporally interleaved (e.g., L0, R0, L1, R1...) or processed in synchronized pairs creates ambiguity in how correlations and edges are formed. The mention of 'cross camera edges' implies a graph edge between left and right views, but this contradicts the earlier temporal graph structure. The system would need modifications to handle spatial (stereo) rather than purely temporal edges, but this is not explained. While the general idea of incorporating stereo via fixed baseline is correct and conventional, the lack of detail on graph topology or temporal indexing makes the actual implementation unclear.

comments.title: Missing index bounds in pose loss equation description
comments.quote: Given a set of ground truth poses _{_ **T** _}[N] i_[and predicted poses] _[ {]_ **[G]** _[}][N] i_[, the pose loss is taken to be the] distance between the ground truth and predicted poses, _Lpose_ =[] _i[||]_[ Log] _SE_ 3[(] **[T]** _[−] i_[1] _·_ **G** _i_ ) _||_ 2.
comments.explanation: The equation appears to use a shorthand for the pose error, but the sum is written as ∑_i without specifying the range of i. From the PAPER SUMMARY, we know the correct form is L_pose = ∑_i ||Log_{SE(3)}(T_{i−1}^{−1} G_i)||², which compares predicted pose G_i to ground truth T_i using the relative transformation from the prior ground truth T_{i-1}. The text version omits the subscript on T and writes T_i rather than T_{i-1}, but this seems to be an OCR or LaTeX rendering error (e.g., 'T_{i−1}' rendered as 'T_i' with a minus hanging). Assuming the most plausible intended symbol, the equation is sound. Additionally, the gauge freedom removal fixes the first two poses, so i probably starts at 2. Since the full definition is present in the summary and standard in the field (relative pose error), the issue resolves in context. No further error remains.

comments.title: Underspecified memory-efficient implementation from RAFT
comments.quote: Storing the full set of correlation volumes would quickly exceed video memory. Instead, we use the memory efficient implementation proposed in RAFT [48].
comments.explanation: This is a reasonable choice—RAFT uses a correlation look-up from a pyramid rather than storing full 4D volumes—but RAFT does not compute dense correlation volumes over long sequences or across arbitrary frame pairs. In DROID-SLAM, the backend processes 'thousands of frames and edges', so simply citing RAFT's method is insufficient. RAFT computes correlation between a pair of images, while DROID-SLAM would need to manage correlations across many keyframe pairs. The text does not explain whether correlations are computed on-demand, cached, or approximated. Furthermore, in Section 3.1, the correlation pyramid is built per edge, so scaling to thousands of edges is computationally demanding. The use of 'memory efficient implementation' is too vague: crucial details like tiling, sparsification, or GPU-aware batching are missing, making reproduction difficult. Given the scale claim (thousands of frames), this omission is significant.

comments.title: Ambiguous role of confidence maps in Mahalanobis distance
comments.quote: Eqn. 4 states that we want an updated pose **G** _[′]_ and depth **d** _[′]_ such that reprojected points match the revised correspondence **p** _[∗] ij_[as predicted by the update operator.] where _∥·∥_ Σ is the Mahalanobis distance which weights the error terms based on the confidence weights **w** _ij_ .
comments.explanation: The PAPER SUMMARY defines w_ij ∈ R^{H×W×2}_+ as a per-pixel confidence map, and the weighted cost in DBA uses Σ diagonal with entries from w_ij. However, optical flow has two components (u,v), so w_ij is 2-channel. The Mahalanobis distance ∥⋅∥_Σ typically uses Σ as covariance, but the text says it is diagonal with entries from w_ij. If w_ij provides two values per pixel, are both used in Σ? The standard treatment would require a 2×2 diagonal block per pixel or a scalar weight. Using two separate weights for u and v components is plausible but unusual; it could break rotational invariance. Alternatively, w_ij might be averaged across channels. The PAPER SUMMARY's cost function does not clarify this. This matters because invalid weighting could affect gradient propagation and robustness. The claim that confidence improves robustness hinges on correct formulation, which is underspecified.

comments.title: Unjustified omission of rolling shutter handling despite noting its challenge
comments.quote: This is a notoriously difficult dataset for monocular methods due to rolling shutter artifacts, motion blur, and heavy rotation.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify rolling shutter as a challenge in the TUM-RGBD dataset, but there is no mention of how DROID-SLAM handles it. Other direct methods (e.g., DSO [15]) explicitly model rolling shutter. DROID-SLAM uses dense correspondence and assumes global shutter in its projection model (implied by Π_c and inverse projection formulas referenced in appendix but not seen). Without modeling shutter or correcting flow estimates, performance on TUM-RGBD may not fairly reflect accuracy. While the system might learn to compensate implicitly through the neural network, the paper makes no claim or ablation regarding this. Given the high accuracy reported, this omission raises concerns about generalization. However, since the system is trained on TartanAir—which may include synthetic rolling shutter effects—and no evidence of failure is presented, the claim may still hold. Still, the lack of justification or discussion is a weakness in methodology reporting.

comments.title: Incorrect Chebyshev Distance Application in Edge Suppression
comments.quote: With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs ||(i, j) − (k, l)||∞ = max(|i − k|, |j − l|).
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems reasonable: Chebyshev distance is a standard metric for grid-based neighborhoods, and using it to suppress spatially adjacent edges in the upper triangle of the N × N distance matrix is logical. However, the issue arises in interpretation—edges in the graph are pairs (i, j) with i < j (since the distance matrix is symmetric and non-co-visible entries are infinity). The Chebyshev distance as defined, max(|i − k|, |j − l|), is being applied to unordered index pairs. But in practice, edge suppression should consider local neighborhoods in the (i, j)-index space, and if both (i, j) and (k, l) are edges (with i < j, k < l), comparing them via coordinate differences in index space requires care. The problem is that the expression ||(i, j) − (k, l)||∞ treats (i, j) as a point in a 2D integer lattice, but the indices are not symmetric: (i, j) and (j, i) are equivalent graph edges but would be far apart in this distance unless symmetry is enforced. However, the text and context suggest only upper-triangular storage (since non-co-visible are set to infinity and edges are sampled by increasing flow), and the Chebyshev ball around (i, j) of radius 2 would include edges (k, l) such that max(|i−k|, |j−l|) ≤ 2. This might oversuppress or misrepresent locality if the graph includes long-range edges, but the intent—local suppression of dense, nearby edges—is clear and contextually justified. While the formulation is slightly ambiguous, it aligns with common pruning practices in BA edge selection. Given the OCR risk and context, this is not a clear error.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Claimed ATE Reduction and Table 4 Values
comments.quote: It successfully tracks all 9 sequences while achieving 83% lower ATE than DeepFactors [9] and which succeeds on all videos and 90% lower ATE than DeepV2D [47].
comments.explanation: The claim of '83% lower ATE than DeepFactors [9]' and '90% lower than DeepV2D' is problematic when checked against Table 4. In TUM-RGBD, our method achieves avg ATE of 0.038 cm. DeepFactors has avg ATE 0.246 cm. The reduction is (0.246 − 0.038)/0.246 ≈ 0.845 or 84.5%, which rounds to ~85%, close to 83%. DeepV2D has 0.602 cm avg; (0.602 − 0.038)/0.602 ≈ 0.937 or 93.7% reduction, not 90%. While these are minor discrepancies, the paper says '83%' and '90%', which are conservative approximations. However, the phrase 'achieves 83% lower ATE than DeepFactors [9] and which succeeds on all videos' is awkward—'which' likely refers to the method itself, but grammar suggests it might refer to DeepFactors, which does not succeed on all videos (fails at least on 'teddy'). Context from the text shows 'Ours' succeeds on all 9, while DeepFactors fails on 'teddy' (value is X), so the comparison is valid only over tracked sequences. The ATE averages listed for DeepFactors and DeepV2D likely include all sequences, some marked 'X'—but in Table 4, 'X' appears in individual rows, while the average row shows non-missing values. For DeepV2D, 'X' appears for 'teddy' and 'xyz' (last two columns), yet average is 0.602. If 'X' means failure, averaging over failed sequences is invalid. But the table shows no separate averaging logic. This suggests either: (1) 'X' indicates missing value not used in average, or (2) authors include imputed or outlier values. Given lack of clarification, the 90% number may be overclaimed if the average includes sequences where DeepV2D failed. While the magnitude trend is correct, the specific percentages are ambiguous and potentially misleading without stating whether averages are over successful runs only. This undermines the precision of the performance claim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in RGB-D Objective Term Implementation
comments.quote: We take our network trained on TartanAir and add an addition term in the optimization objective penalizing the distance between the predicted inv. depth and inv. depth measured by the sensor.
comments.explanation: The statement describes a key adaptation for RGB-D input but leaves critical implementation details unspecified. Specifically: Is this term added during training (fine-tuning) or only at inference? The context says 'without any finetuning', so it must be added only at inference. But how is the term incorporated into the DBA layer? The depth estimation is inverse depth; is the error in inverse depth space, or in metric depth? The sensor often returns noisy depth, especially at boundaries or on reflective surfaces. Is there a weighting (e.g., by confidence or mask) applied? How is the depth preprocessed (e.g., median filtering, invalid removal)? Moreover, the term is claimed to be added 'in the optimization objective'—but DBA minimizes geometric reprojection error with flow revisions. Is the sensor depth term added directly to the cost in Eqn. 4? If so, the term would be ||d_pred − d_sensor||, but this couples depth directly without reprojection, potentially conflicting with flow-based photometric consistency. For the system to remain consistent, this term should be reprojection-based or carefully balanced. The lack of formative equation or weight setting (e.g., λ_sensor) is a significant underspecification. While the paper notes robustness, a reader cannot reproduce this modality switch without more detail. This is especially important because the claim of generalization without retraining hinges on this plug-in term.

comments.title: Inconsistent Terminology: 'foor' Instead of 'floor'
comments.explanation: In the row of sequence names just above Table 4: 'desk', 'desk2', 'foor', 'plant', etc., 'foor' is almost certainly a typo for 'floor', likely due to OCR or transcription error. This is not a technical error per se, but it could mislead a careful reader trying to align results with known TUM-RGBD sequences (e.g., 'floor', a common one). All other entries are valid. Given that the rest of the table values are numerically sound and 'foor' appears only once, and that context clearly refers to TUM-RGBD sequences, this is likely a notation artifact. No further inconsistency follows from it. The average is computed correctly. Thus, while it introduces ambiguity, context resolves it. OCR note: 'foor' → 'floor' is highly plausible.

comments.title: Overstatement of Real-Time Performance on TartanAir
comments.quote: On TartanAir, due to much faster camera motion, we are unable to run in real-time, averaging 8fps. However, this is still a 16x speedup over the top 2 submissions to the TartanAir SLAM challenge, which rely on COLMAP [40].
comments.explanation: The issue lies in the implication that '8fps' is meaningfully compared to '16x speedup over COLMAP'. COLMAP is a structure-from-motion tool, typically used offline and not frame-rate limited. The 'top 2 submissions' likely ran COLMAP in batch mode over entire sequences, which can take hours. So a 16x speedup—even at 8fps, which is below video rate—can be valid. However, the term 'speedup' without specifying baseline time (e.g., wall-clock vs. video duration) is ambiguous. Moreover, calling 8fps 'unable to run in real-time' is accurate (video typically >15–30fps), but the comparison might mislead readers into thinking DROID-SLAM is dramatically faster in online performance, when in fact it's a comparison to offline optimization. The authors correctly say 'we are unable to run in real-time', so they don't overclaim that. However, the phrasing '16x speedup' in a real-time SLAM context may lead readers to believe it achieves real-time gains, which it does not on TartanAir. While not technically incorrect, the claim is framed in a way that could exaggerate practical advantage.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The provided introduction is exemplary in its clarity, structure, and academic positioning. The authors efficiently bridge the gap between classical geometric SLAM and deep learning, setting up a compelling narrative that shifts the paradigm from "learning-based methods vs. classical benchmarks" to a unified, hybrid approach. The technical contributions—specifically the distinction between "dense" bundle adjustment and the subspace-based approaches of predecessors like BA-Net—are articulated with technical precision. The most significant strength of the writing is the clear articulation of empirical superiority supported by quantified, high-impact claims (e.g., the 82% error reduction on EuRoC). If there is a potential pitfall in the paper’s scope, it lies in the ambitious claim of "zero failures" across multiple datasets; the authors must ensure that their definition of "failure" is rigorous and transparent in the experimental section, as such strong claims are often the primary target of scrutiny during peer review. Overall, the foundational text is technically mature and effectively justifies the novelty of the DROID architecture.

comments.title: Unclear Modeling Capacity Comparison
comments.quote: In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: The text contains a logical error where it claims to borrow 'greater modeling capacity' from 'indirect approaches', immediately after characterizing those same indirect approaches as having limited information compared to direct methods. The authors likely intended to contrast their method against indirect methods by citing direct modeling capacity, but the phrasing confuses the reader.

comments.title: Mathematical under-specification of Dense Bundle Adjustment
comments.quote: We define the cost function over the entire frame graph
comments.explanation: The section describing the Dense Bundle Adjustment (DBA) layer references a 'cost function' and terms like 'Hessian matrix' and 'Schur complement', but fails to provide the formal mathematical expression for the cost function itself. This makes the subsequent derivations regarding optimization difficult to verify.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Correspondence Notation
comments.quote: We denote the corrected correspondence as $p^* _{ij} = r _{ij} + p _{ij}$
comments.explanation: The notation uses the same symbol ($p_{ij}$) to represent both the initial optical flow projection and the corrected correspondence within the same operation. This creates a circular reference that is prone to confusion, despite the likely intent (p* = delta + p_initial).

comments.title: Ambiguous Confidence Map Dimensions
comments.quote: associated confidence map $w_{ij} \in R^{H \times W \times 2}$
comments.explanation: Defining the confidence map $w_{ij}$ as a 3D tensor of size $R^{H \times W \times 2}$ is ambiguous for a weight applied to 2D reprojection errors in a Mahalanobis distance. It is unclear if these two components represent diagonal covariance elements, x/y-specific weights, or another geometric interpretation, which affects the validity of the Mahalanobis distance calculation.

comments.title: Under-specification of Damping Factor Application
comments.quote: predict a pixel-wise damping factor $\lambda$
comments.explanation: The text describes using a pixel-wise damping factor $\lambda$ for the Schur complement, but does not clarify whether this is a scalar applied to the identity matrix or a diagonal matrix of values. While technically consistent with Levenberg-Marquardt, the specific implementation of the damping update is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Odometry Only' Evaluation
comments.quote: Ours (odometry only)†
comments.explanation: It is unclear if the 'odometry only' result involves a modification to the model architecture (e.g., removing the global backend) or simply a change in inference mode. Given the paper's focus on the Dense Bundle Adjustment, this architectural distinction is necessary to interpret the ablation results.

comments.title: Vague logical criteria for Chebyshev graph suppression
comments.quote: where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs || ( i, j ) - ( k, l ) ||∞ = max( |i - k|, |j - l| ).
comments.explanation: While Chebyshev distance is a standard metric, the criteria for 'suppressing' neighboring edges in the frame graph using this distance is mathematically vague. It is unclear if the suppression is a spatial grid filter or a temporal clustering heuristic, leaving the graph pruning logic underspecified.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Representative Capacity
comments.quote: This is an easier optimization problem and avoids the need for more complicated representations such as image pyramids. In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: The text states that the proposed approach borrows 'the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches' from indirect approaches, yet the paragraph immediately preceding this sentence characterizes these same 'indirect approaches' as using only 'corners and edges' (limited information) compared to direct methods. It seems likely the authors intended to compare their method to direct methods in the second instance, but as written, it confuses the reader regarding whether the modeling capacity claim refers to the direct or indirect category.

comments.title: Unspecified cost function for Dense Bundle Adjustment
comments.quote:
We define the cost function over the entire frame graph

where $\lVert\cdot\rVert_ \Sigma$ is the Mahalanobis distance which weights the error terms based on the confidence weights $w_{ij}$
comments.explanation: The text references a cost function (immediately following the phrase 'We define the cost function over the entire frame graph') but the actual mathematical expression for this cost function is missing from the provided passage. While the surrounding text describes the goal (reprojected points matching revised correspondence), the lack of the formal summation notation/equation makes the subsequent mention of the 'Hessian matrix' and 'Schur complement' difficult to verify, as the specific objective function is the foundation for these derivations. Although the appendix is mentioned to contain formulas, the lack of the core objective function in section 3.2 makes the description of the DBA layer mathematically underspecified.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in confidence map w_ij
comments.quote: associated confidence map $w_{ij} \in R^{H \times W \times 2}$
comments.explanation: The confidence map $w_{ij}$ is intended to weight the error terms in the Mahalanobis distance defined in the (missing) cost function. Typically, a confidence weight for a 2D reprojection error would either be a scalar per pixel (to weight the magnitude of the error) or a 2x2 matrix per pixel (to account for anisotropic uncertainty covariance). Defining $w_{ij}$ as $R^{H \times W \times 2}$ suggests a vector of size 2, which is potentially ambiguous (is it diagonal elements of a covariance? x/y components?). Given this is used in a Mahalanobis distance, the dimension 2 is geometrically unusual for a standard weight unless it implies a very specific diagonal approximation.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding damping factor lambda
comments.quote: predict a pixel-wise damping factor $\lambda$. ... system can be solved efficiently using the Schur complement with the pixelwise damping factor $\lambda$ added to the depth block
comments.explanation: The text states that the system is solved using the Schur complement, which is the standard procedure in Bundle Adjustment to marginalize out 3D points (or in this case, inverse depths) to solve for poses efficiently. However, the mention of adding a pixelwise damping factor $\lambda$ to the depth block—which is diagonal and 'cheaply inverted'—refers to a Levenberg-Marquardt style modification. It is unclear if $\lambda$ is a scalar applied to the identity matrix of the depth block or a diagonal matrix of values. While technically consistent with standard optimization, the implementation detail of the damping application is underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dense correspondence definition
comments.quote: p_{ij} = r_{ij} + p_{ij}
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define the corrected correspondence as p*_{ij} = r_{ij} + p_{ij}. However, in the 'Key Equations' summary (provided in the prompt context) and the text in Section 3.2, p_{ij} is defined as the optical flow projection. If p_{ij} is a coordinate, adding r_{ij} (which is a revision flow field) is dimensionally correct (pixels + pixels = pixels), but the notation p*_{ij} = r_{ij} + p_{ij} uses the same symbol p_{ij} on both sides of the equation with different meanings (original vs. corrected). Given the context of p*_{ij} = r_{ij} + p_{ij} being the 'revised correspondence', it creates a circular reference. While the intent is clear (p* = delta + p_initial), the notation is prone to confusion.

comments.title: Unspecified definition of Chebyshev distance in graph construction
comments.quote: where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs || ( i, j ) − ( k, l ) ||∞ = max( |i − k|, |j − l| ).
comments.explanation: The authors define the Chebyshev distance for index pairs (i, j) and (k, l) to suppress edges in the frame graph. While this is a standard metric, the application here is to suppress 'neighboring edges'. If an edge is defined as a tuple of indices (i, j) representing a connection between frame i and frame j, the Chebyshev distance between two tuples (i, j) and (k, l) is well-defined. However, the logic for 'suppressing' neighboring edges within a distance of 2 as defined by this metric is underspecified. Does this imply a spatial filter on the 2D grid matrix of the graph? It is slightly ambiguous whether the distance is meant to prevent redundancy in locally dense temporal clusters, and while the intent is understandable for graph pruning, the criteria for 'suppression' is mathematically vague.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'odometry only' evaluation
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the authors include a row labeled 'Ours (odometry only)†'. The term 'odometry' in SLAM usually implies a local tracking window without global loop closure or map-wide optimization. Given that the paper emphasizes the 'Dense Bundle Adjustment' and 'Global Backend' as core innovations, it is unclear if the 'odometry only' result is simply the frontend running without the backend, or a specific architectural ablation. The dagger symbol (†) refers to the caption 'denotes visual odometry methods', but it's not explicitly confirmed if the proposed model is modified (e.g., removing the backend) or just evaluated in a mode that doesn't use the full global optimization. While context hints at this, the distinction between the baseline architecture and the 'odometry only' variant is not formally defined.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in distance matrix definition
comments.quote: where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs $|| (i, j) − (k, l) ||∞ = max( |i − k|, |j − l| )
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4 (Backend), the distance is defined for index pairs $(i, j)$ and $(k, l)$. Given that $i$ and $j$ represent frame indices from the frame graph (of dimension $N \times N$), this definition uses 4 indices for a 2-tuple operation. While the formula $max(|i-k|, |j-l|)$ is standard for Chebyshev distance between 2D points $(i, j)$ and $(k, l)$, it is mathematically ambiguous to refer to them as 'index pairs' in this context without clarifying if they represent matrix entries or coordinates. In the paper summary provided by the prompt, the Chebyshev distance was defined as $||(i, j) - (k, l)||\infty = max(|i - k|, |j - l|)$, which aligns with the text, but the terminology 'index pairs' is potentially confusing as it could refer to the edge $(i,j)$ being a single index in the set $E$.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'revised correspondence'
comments.quote: We denote the corrected correspondence as $p^* _{ij} = r _{ij} + p _{ij}$
comments.explanation: In the summary provided by the prompt, the revised correspondence is defined as $p^*_{ij} = r_{ij} + p_{ij}$. Later in Section 3.2, the text states $p^* _{ij} = r _{ij} + p _{ij}$. However, immediately preceding this, the prompt summary mentions a dependency on $d'_i$. While $p_{ij}$ is defined as a correspondence field derived from $G$ and $d$, the update operator predicts $r_{ij}$ to correct the *correspondence*. If $r_{ij}$ is an additive flow revision to the correspondence, it implies a pixel-wise displacement. This appears consistent, but the transition between the geometric definition (using the camera model $\Pi_c$) and the learned residual $r_{ij}$ is slightly underspecified regarding how the DBA layer reconciles a potentially inconsistent $p^*_{ij}$ with the rigid body constraints.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy in ETH-3D success rate
comments.quote: On the test set, we successfully track 30/32 RGB-D, improving over the next best of 19/32.
comments.explanation: In the concluding paragraph of the 'ETH3D-SLAM' subsection, the author claims to track 30/32 datasets. However, looking at the provided text Figure 4, the right-hand plot shows the peak 'successful datasets' for 'Ours' reaching 30, but the text earlier in the abstract and Figure 4 description labels the comparison as 30 vs 19. While internally consistent within the paper, it is common to clarify if the 30/32 refers to the test set specifically or the entire benchmark suite. The phrasing 'track 30/32 RGB-D' is slightly ambiguous as to whether those are specifically the test set or the combined set (train+test).

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a compelling and timely contribution to visual SLAM by introducing a differentiable, recurrent architecture that unifies camera pose and dense depth estimation through a novel Dense Bundle Adjustment layer. The writing is generally clear and logically structured, effectively positioning the work within both classical optimization and deep-learning SLAM traditions while articulating three well-defined claims regarding accuracy, robustness, and cross-modal generalization. The reported benchmark results are highly impressive and strongly motivate the proposed architectural design. However, the paper would benefit from tempering some of the assertive, marketing-style phrasing (e.g., “very large margins,” “zero failures”) with more precise statistical reporting and transparent failure-mode analysis. A significant concern is the bold claim of training exclusively on synthetic TartanAir data while achieving state-of-the-art performance on diverse real-world datasets; this sim-to-real generalization warrants deeper discussion of domain shift, evaluation protocol rigor, and potential dataset-specific biases. Additionally, while the differentiable optimization formulation is conceptually well-motivated, the current description lacks sufficient attention to computational complexity, memory scalability, and real-time feasibility—critical considerations for practical SLAM deployment. Addressing these points with more methodological transparency and rigorous empirical grounding will substantially strengthen the paper’s academic credibility and practical relevance.

comments.title: Geometrically inconsistent optical flow difference formula
comments.quote: Additionally, we use the correspondence field to derive optical flow induced by camera motion as the difference **p** _ij −_ **p** _j_ .
comments.explanation: The authors correctly define p_i as a pixel coordinate grid in frame i and p_ij as the mapping of p_i into frame j using the current pose and depth estimates. Standard optical flow represents the displacement of a tracked point across frames, which should be p_ij − p_i (or p_i − p_ij depending on sign convention). The stated formula p_ij − p_j subtracts the j-th frame's coordinate grid from the projected coordinates, which does not yield a meaningful motion field and breaks geometrically with the prior definitions. This appears to be a typographical error (likely p_i was intended). If taken literally, the expression would not represent camera-induced motion and would mislead implementation. I flag it as a technical inconsistency that should be corrected to maintain alignment with the correspondence field definitions.

comments.title: Contradictory attribution of modeling capacity in direct vs. indirect comparison
comments.quote: In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the final sentence attributing 'greater modeling capacity' to indirect approaches. Upon carefully re-reading the paragraph, I checked the preceding sentences and found that the authors explicitly set up the opposite contrast: they state that direct methods 'use the full image, allowing us to leverage a wider range of information than indirect methods with typically only use corners and edges.' In standard visual SLAM literature, direct approaches are indeed recognized for exploiting dense intensity and gradient information across the entire image, which provides greater modeling capacity than the discrete feature extraction used by indirect methods. The concluding sentence appears to be a typographical or copy-paste slip, mistakenly repeating 'indirect approaches' instead of 'direct approaches.' While this does not undermine the technical methodology or equations, it creates a localized logical inconsistency in the Related Work overview that momentarily contradicts the authors' own framing. The issue is moderate because it occurs in an introductory/overview paragraph where authors often simplify or gloss over details, and it does not affect the core technical claims or reproducibility.

comments.title: Imprecise description of monocular gauge fixing
comments.quote: We solve this problem by fixing the first two poses to the ground-truth poses of each training sequence. Fixing the first pose removes the 6-dof gauge freedom. Fixing the second pose resolves the scale freedom.
comments.explanation: In monocular bundle adjustment, gauge freedom corresponds to a 7-DOF similarity transform (3 rotation, 3 translation, 1 scale). Fixing the first pose indeed removes 6 DOF, leaving only scale. However, the authors state that 'fixing the second pose resolves the scale freedom.' Strictly speaking, fixing an entire SE(3) pose would fix 6 additional DOF, which would overconstrain the optimization system (totaling 12 fixed DOF) and likely cause conflicts with the differentiable update operator. The authors' intent is clearly to anchor the scale parameter (and possibly the translation direction) using the relative pose to the second frame, not to fully fix the second pose as a boundary condition. A careful reader might misinterpret this as fixing 6 DOF for the second frame, which would be mathematically inconsistent with the claim that only scale remains. The phrasing should specify that only the scale (and possibly direction) of the second frame's translation is fixed to resolve the remaining 1 DOF.

comments.title: Minor typographical error affecting phrasing
comments.quote: We take our network trained on TartanAir and add an addition term in the optimization objective penalizing the distance between the predicted inv. depth and inv. depth measured by the sensor.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'add an addition term' is clearly a typo for 'add an additional term'. It does not affect the technical meaning or reproducibility of the RGB-D extension, but it is worth noting for final editing. The rest of the sentence correctly describes the depth-penalty loss introduced in Section 3.4, which matches the assumption and claim that stereo/RGB-D inputs are handled by fixing inter-camera poses and adding a depth prediction penalty term.

The repetition breaks the logical conclusion of the paragraph and introduces an ambiguity that contradicts the established definitions. It reads as a drafting/typographical error that should be corrected to 'direct approaches' in the second clause.

comments.title: Slight imprecision in Hessian structure description
comments.quote: Since each term in Eqn. 4 only includes a single depth variable, the Hessian matrix has block diagonal structure.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that each reprojection error term depends on only one depth variable, which is the key property that enables Schur complement decomposition. However, stating that 'the Hessian matrix has block diagonal structure' is slightly imprecise: only the depth-depth submatrix is diagonal. The full Hessian over poses and depths is block-sparse (with dense pose-pose blocks and zero off-diagonal depth-depth entries). This is common shorthand in SLAM literature, but a reader looking for exact matrix structure might be momentarily confused. The context of applying the Schur complement immediately after clarifies the intent, so this does not undermine the method, but precision would be improved.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: As an expert academic reviewer, I find this paper to be a strong and well-motivated contribution to the field of visual SLAM, with clear writing and impressive quantitative results that convincingly demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness across multiple datasets and sensor modalities. The core ideas—recurrent iterative updates inspired by RAFT combined with a differentiable dense bundle adjustment layer—are novel and elegantly bridge classical optimization and deep learning, and the authors' emphasis on zero-shot generalization from monocular training to stereo/RGB-D test-time input is particularly compelling. However, I see several significant issues that temper the overall impact. First, the claim of “high robustness” is based almost entirely on synthetic training data (TartanAir); generalization to real-world scenes with drastically different appearance, dynamics, and lighting is not thoroughly probed, and the catastrophic failure rate on ETH-3D (2 out of 32 sequences) still warrants deeper analysis. Second, the paper’s novelty relative to RAFT and prior works like DeepV2D and BA-Net is asserted but not rigorously delineated—specifically, the architectural contribution of the dense per-pixel optimization in the BA layer versus a learned basis remains to be shown as essential via ablation, and the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of failure modes (e.g., when does the flow estimator fail and propagate errors?). Finally, while the results are impressive, the evaluation is heavily benchmark-focused; there is little qualitative insight or analysis of the learned representations, and the reliance on a single synthetic training set raises questions about dataset bias and real-world deployment. Overall, this is a high-quality paper with clear exposition, but these gaps in generalizability and architectural justification should be addressed to fully solidify its contributions.

comments.title: Erroneous attribution of 'greater modeling capacity' to indirect approaches instead of direct
comments.quote: In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims to combine strengths of direct and indirect methods, but repeats 'indirect approaches' for both the smoother objective function and the greater modeling capacity. The preceding discussion clearly states that direct methods have greater modeling capacity while indirect methods have a smoother objective. The repetition is almost certainly a typo; the second instance should read 'direct approaches'. This error misrepresents the claimed advantage and could mislead a careful reader about the method's novelty.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of indirect methods: 'only' corners and edges
comments.quote: allowing us to leverage a wider range of information than indirect methods with typically only use corners and edges.
comments.explanation: The clause 'with typically only use corners and edges' contains a grammatical error ('with' should be 'which'). More importantly, the claim that indirect methods 'typically only use corners and edges' is an oversimplification. Many indirect SLAM systems use image patches around keypoints that capture more than just corner locations. Describing them as 'only' using geometric corners and edges is misleading, though the broader point (indirect methods are sparse) is valid.

comments.title: Underspecified edge sampling stopping criterion in backend
comments.quote: We then sample new edges from the distance matrix in order of increasing flow. With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs || (i, j) - (k, l) ||∞ = max(|i−k|, |j−l|). We then apply the update operator to the entire frame graph, often consisting of thousands of frames and edges.
comments.explanation: The backend description explains how new edges are sampled from the distance matrix but never specifies the stopping condition for sampling. How many edges are added? Are edges sampled until a target number is reached, until the graph becomes fully connected, or based on a flow threshold? Without this detail, the procedure cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Underspecified motion-only bundle adjustment for non-keyframes
comments.quote: In order to recover the poses of non-keyframes, we perform motion-only bundle adjustment by iteratively estimating flow between each keyframe and its neighboring non-keyframes.
comments.explanation: The description of how non-keyframe poses are recovered is vague. 'Iteratively estimating flow' does not specify which algorithm is used, how many iterations are performed, how 'neighboring' non-keyframes are defined, and whether depths are kept fixed or also optimized. This level of detail is insufficient for reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about ORB-SLAM3 results on TUM-RGBD
comments.quote: Our method is both robust and accurate. It successfully tracks all 9 sequences while achieving 83% lower ATE than DeepFactors [9] and which succeeds on all videos and 90% lower ATE than DeepV2D [47].
comments.explanation: The text before Table 4 states 'Classical SLAM algorithms such as ORB-SLAM tend to fail on most of the sequences.' However, Table 4 shows ORB-SLAM3 has actual ATE values for several sequences and only fails on 5 out of 9. The claim 'fail on most sequences' is an overstatement and could mislead readers about the actual failure rate.

comments.title: Underspecified RGB-D extension: missing details on depth penalty term
comments.quote: add an addition term in the optimization objective penalizing the distance between the predicted inv. depth and inv. depth measured by the sensor
comments.explanation: The description of how RGB-D measurements are incorporated is extremely vague. Neither the exact form of the penalty (L1, L2, Huber?), how sensor noise/uncertainty is handled, whether depth remains a variable or is partially fixed, nor the weight or scaling factor for the penalty term are specified. For a published NeurIPS paper, this level of detail is insufficient for reproducibility and constitutes a major gap for a claimed contribution.
